focus: Cross-study meta-analysis statistics applied to cancer genomics / mutation-frequency aggregation. Methods for pooling per-study gene-level mutation ratios across heterogeneous cBioPortal-style studies. Anchors: DerSimonian-Laird, REML/metafor, GLMM (Stijnen/Nyaga), Freeman-Tukey arcsine vs logit (Barendregt vs Lin & Xu), Hartung-Knapp-Sidik-Jonkman, Higgins-Thompson I-squared, rare-event/zero-cell handling, and cancer-genomics-native consensus aggregation (IntOGen).

queries.text: meta-analysis pooling proportions Freeman-Tukey arcsine logit
Hartung Knapp random effects meta-analysis adjustment
meta-analysis rare events Mantel-Haenszel Peto odds ratio
Sidik Jonkman tau squared estimator meta-analysis
generalized linear mixed model meta-analysis proportions binomial

run_metadata.notes: curl blocked in sandbox; PubMed and OpenAlex accessed via WebFetch tool. Most PubMed hits for the broad queries were off-topic clinical meta-analyses; shortlist is restricted to methodology papers (plus IntOGen as the cancer-genomics-native aggregation reference).

candidates.title: Meta-analysis in clinical trials
candidates.rationale: Seminal random-effects estimator; default option in metafor when method='DL'. Must be cited when we switch away from naive pooling.

candidates.title: Conducting Meta-Analyses in R with the metafor Package
candidates.rationale: Canonical metafor manual — defines rma, rma.mh, rma.glmm, escalc; lists DL/REML/PM/SJ/HE/ML/EB estimators side-by-side. Primary tool-of-record if we adopt meta-analytic pooling in R.

candidates.title: A comparison of heterogeneity variance estimators in simulated random-effects meta-analyses
candidates.rationale: Simulation comparison of DL, REML, PM, SJ, HE, ML, EB tau-squared estimators. Recommends REML (with HKSJ for K<30) as general default — key modern guidance for which random-effects method to use.

candidates.title: Random effects meta-analysis of event outcome in the framework of the generalized linear mixed model with applications in sparse data
candidates.rationale: Exact binomial / beta-binomial GLMM for sparse event meta-analysis — principled alternative to continuity corrections and transforms when per-study mutation counts are small or zero. Maps to rma.glmm(measure='PLO') for this project's per-study (mutated, sequenced) counts.

candidates.title: Arcsine-based transformations for meta-analysis of proportions: Pros, cons, and alternatives
candidates.rationale: 2020 critique of Freeman-Tukey arcsine for meta-analysis of proportions; argues for GLMM (logit link) and Bayesian alternatives over arcsine transforms. Replaces Barendregt 2013 as the current-generation default recommendation for pooling proportions.

candidates.venue: Journal of Epidemiology and Community Health
candidates.title: Meta-analysis of prevalence
candidates.rationale: Canonical Freeman-Tukey double-arcsine paper for pooling prevalences. Context read for the Lin & Xu 2020 critique; still widely used in practice. Know why we should NOT default to it. (OpenAlex cited_by stale; actual citation count is thousands.)

candidates.title: Metaprop: a Stata command to perform meta-analysis of binomial data
candidates.rationale: De-facto reference for meta-analysis of single proportions (no control arm) — exactly the 'per-study mutation-ratio' problem. Covers arcsine vs logit, Wilson/exact CIs, multiple heterogeneity methods. R equivalent: meta::metaprop.

candidates.title: Quantifying heterogeneity in a meta-analysis
candidates.rationale: Seminal I-squared paper. Required citation for any heterogeneity diagnostic we report alongside pooled gene-cancer estimates.

candidates.title: Plea for routinely presenting prediction intervals in meta-analysis
candidates.rationale: Argues prediction intervals (not just pooled CIs) should be routine in random-effects meta-analysis. Directly relevant for reporting per-gene cross-study mutation-rate prediction intervals. Widely cited HKSJ practical-guidance paper.

candidates.title: A compendium of mutational cancer driver genes
candidates.rationale: IntOGen 2020 — cancer-genomics-native consensus-voting pipeline aggregating mutations across ~28,000 samples from ~66 cohorts for driver discovery. Sits between naive pooling and random-effects meta-analysis. Essential cross-reference when describing what pipeline-level cancer-genomics aggregation currently looks like and why random-effects is an upgrade.

candidates.title: The impact of continuity correction methods in Cochrane reviews with single-zero trials with rare events: A meta-epidemiological study
candidates.rationale: Empirical 2024 reassessment of 0.5 continuity correction vs alternatives in sparse / zero-event meta-analysis. Calibrates expectations for 'per-study mutation count is 0 for this gene' rows.

candidates.title: Network meta-analysis of rare events using the Mantel-Haenszel method
candidates.rationale: MH method for rare events generalised to network meta-analysis. Useful if we eventually attempt cross-cohort gene-level comparisons rather than single-arm prevalence pooling.

candidates.title: Meta-analysis of rare events under the assumption of a homogeneous treatment effect
candidates.rationale: Formalizes the 'common true effect' framing for rare-event meta-analysis — useful for contrasting with the random-effects assumption when per-study gene rates are genuinely heterogeneous vs just noisy.

gaps: HKSJ primary sources (Hartung 2001, Sidik & Jonkman 2002/2005) not retrieved as individual records — covered indirectly by Langan 2018 and IntHout 2016.
Paule-Mandel (1982) primary paper not retrieved; covered by Langan 2018.
dmetar / 'Doing Meta-Analysis in R' handbook (Harrer, Cuijpers, Furukawa, Ebert 2021, CRC Press) not in OpenAlex (book). Record manually as @book in references.bib if dmetar enters the pipeline.

Bayesian hierarchical pooling of cancer mutation frequencies (brms/rstanarm/Stan): no dedicated genomics-application paper found. Closest is Stijnen 2010 as methods foundation; genuine research-application gap.

Genomics applications of per-gene random-effects meta-analysis to somatic-mutation frequencies: essentially zero published examples. IntOGen (Martinez-Jimenez 2020) and Bailey 2018 are closest analogues (consensus voting, not random-effects). Confirms the 'largest methodology gap' framing — strong opportunity for this project to produce per-gene pooled rates with tau-squared / I-squared / prediction intervals.

recommended_default.approach: GLMM with logit link on per-study (mutated, sequenced) counts — metafor::rma.glmm(measure='PLO', method='ML') or equivalent Stan/brms random-intercept binomial model (Stijnen 2010; Lin & Xu 2020; Nyaga 2014).
reports_alongside: pooled estimate with 95% CI under HKSJ adjustment when K<30 studies per gene-cancer pair
disclosed_alternatives: Freeman-Tukey double-arcsine (Barendregt 2013) — disclose only, do not default.
IntOGen-style consensus voting (Martinez-Jimenez 2020) — complementary view, not a replacement.
recommended_default.rationale: GLMM-based pooling handles zero counts natively, respects the binomial data-generating process, produces principled heterogeneity statistics, and scales to thousands of genes without the distortions introduced by arcsine transforms on proportions near 0 or 1.
